test_text.title: Opportunities for voluntary work in Southoe village

audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a man asking about voluntary work in the village that he has just moved to.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Jane Fairbanks speaking.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: My name is Frank Pritchard.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: I've just retired and moved to Southoe.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: I'd like to become a volunteer.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: And I gather you coordinate voluntary work in the village.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: What sort of thing could I do?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Well, we need help with the village library.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: We borrow books from the town library, and individuals also donate them.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: So one thing you could do is get involved in collecting them.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: If you've got a car, that is.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Yes, that's no problem.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: The times are pretty flexible, so we can arrange it to suit you.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, another thing is the records that we keep of the books we're given.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And those we borrow and need to return to the town library, it would be very useful to have another person to help keep them up to date.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: I'm used to working on a computer, I presume they're computerized?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Is the library purpose built?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: I haven't noticed it when I've walked around the village.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: No, we simply have the use of a room in the village hall, the West Room.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It's on the left as you go in.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I must go and have a look inside the hall.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Yes, it's a nice building.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Do you run a lunch club in the village for elderly people?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: I know a lot of places do.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Yes, we have a very successful club.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I could help with transport, if that's of any use.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Oh, definitely.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: People come to the club from neighboring villages, and we're always in need of more drivers.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And does the club have groups that focus on a particular hobby too?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: I could get involved in one or two, particularly if there are any art groups.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Excellent.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I'll find out where we need help and get back to you.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: What about help for individual residents?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Do you arrange that at all?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Yes, we do it as a one-off.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Oh, in fact, there's Mrs Carroll.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: She needs a lift to the hospital next week, and we're struggling to find someone.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: When's her appointment?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: On Tuesday, it would take the whole morning.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I could do that.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Oh, that would be great.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And also next week, we're arranging to have some work done to Mr Selsbury's house before he moves, as he isn't healthy enough to do it himself.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: We've got some people to decorate his kitchen.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: But if you could do some weeding in his garden, that would be wonderful.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: OK, I'd enjoy that, and presumably the day and time are flexible.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Just say when would suit you best, and we'll let Mr Selsbury know.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The volunteers group also organizes monthly social events, which is a great way to meet other people, of course.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: So, next month on the 19th of October, we're holding a quiz.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: A couple of residents are great at planning unusual ones, and we always fill the village hall.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: That sounds like fun.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Can I do anything to help?
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Well, because of the number of people, we need plenty of refreshments for halfway through.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: So, if you could provide any, we'd be grateful.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I'm sure I could.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: I'll think about what to make and let you know.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Then on November the 18th.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: We're holding a dance, also in the village hall.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: We've booked a band that specializes in music of the 1930s - they've been before.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And we've had a lot of requests to bring them back.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: I'm not really a dancer, but I'd like to do something to help.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Well, we sell tickets in advance.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: And having an extra person to check them at the door as people arrive would be good.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It can be quite a bottleneck if everyone arrives at once.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: OK, I'm happy with that.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Oh. We're also arranging a New Year's Eve party.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: We're expecting that to be a really big event, so instead of the village hall, it'll be held in the Mountfort hotel.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: The mountfort, MOUNTFORT Hotel.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: It isn't in Southoe itself, but it's only a couple of miles away.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: The hotel will be providing dinner, and we've booked a band.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: The one thing we haven't got yet is a poster.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: That isn't something you could do by any chance, is it?
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Well, actually, yes.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Uh. Before I retired, I was a graphic designer, so that's right up my street.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: Perfect, I'll give you the details, and then perhaps you could send me a draft.
audioscript.speaker: JANE

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: FRANK

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Opportunities for voluntary work in Southoe village

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a guide at a tourist attraction called Oniton Hall talking to a group of visitors.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, and welcome to Oniton Hall, one of the largest estates in the area.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: My name's Nick, and I'm one of the guides.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: I'll give you a brief introduction to the estate while you're sitting down, and then we'll walk round.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: The estate consists of the house, gardens, park land and farm, and it dates back to the 14th century.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: The original house was replaced in the late 17th century.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: And of course, it has had a large number of owners.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Almost all of them have left their mark, generally by adding new rooms.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Like the ballroom and conservatory, or by demolishing others.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: The farm looks much as it's always done, although the current owner has done a great deal of work to the flower beds.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: In the 17th century, the estate was owned by a very wealthy man called Sir Edward Downes.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: His intention was to escape from the world of politics.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: After years as an active politician, and to build a new house worthy of his big collection of books, paintings and sculptures.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: He broke off contact with his former political allies and hosted meetings of creative and literary people, like painters and poets.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Unusually for his time, he didn't care whether his guests were rich or poor, as long as they had talent.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Big houses like Oniton had dozens of servants until the 1920s or 30s, and we've tried to show what their working lives were like.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Photographs of course don't give much of an idea, so instead, as you go round the house, you'll see volunteers dressed up as nineteenth-century servants, going about their work.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: They'll explain what they're doing, and tell you their recipes, or what tools they're using.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: We've just introduced this feature to replace the audio guide.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: We used to have available.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: I see there are a number of children here with you today.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Well, we have several activities specially for children, like dressing up in the sorts of clothes that children wore in the past.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: And as it's a fine day, some of you will probably want to play in the adventure playground.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Our latest addition is child-sized tractors, that you can drive around the grounds.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We'll also be going into the farm, that's part of the estate, where there's plenty to do.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Most of the buildings date from the 18th century.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: So you can really step back into an agricultural past.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Until recently, the dairy was where milk from the cows was turned into cheese.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: It's now the place to go for lunch, or afternoon tea, or just a cup of coffee, and a slice of homemade cake.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: The big stone building that dominates the farm is the large barn, and in here is our collection of agricultural tools.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: These were used in the past to plough the earth, sow seeds, make gates, and much more.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: There's a small barn, also made of stone, where you can groom the donkeys and horses to keep their coats clean.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: They really seem to enjoy having it done.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: And children love grooming them.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: The horses no longer live in the stables, which instead is the place to go to buy gifts, books, our own jams and pickles.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: And clothes and blankets made of wool from our sheep.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: Outside the shed, which is the only brick building, you can climb into a horse-drawn carriage for a lovely relaxing tour of the park and farm.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: The carriages are well over 100 years old.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: And finally, the parkland, which was laid out in the 18th century, with a lake and trees that are now well established.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: You'll see types of cattle and sheep that are hardly ever found on farms these days.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: We're helping to preserve them to stop their numbers falling further.
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: OK, well, if you'd like to come with me, we will start...
audioscript.speaker: NICK

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: I see there are a number of children here with you today.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: Well, we have several activities specially for children, like dressing up in the sorts of clothes that children wore in the past.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: And as it's a fine day, some of you will probably want to play in the adventure playground.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: Our latest addition is child-sized tractors, that you can drive around the grounds.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: We'll also be going into the farm, that's part of the estate, where there's plenty to do.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: Most of the buildings date from the 18th century.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: So you can really step back into an agricultural past.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: Until recently, the dairy was where milk from the cows was turned into cheese.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: It's now the place to go for lunch, or afternoon tea, or just a cup of coffee, and a slice of homemade cake.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: The big stone building that dominates the farm is the large barn, and in here is our collection of agricultural tools.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: These were used in the past to plough the earth, sow seeds, make gates, and much more.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: There's a small barn, also made of stone, where you can groom the donkeys and horses to keep their coats clean.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: They really seem to enjoy having it done.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: And children love grooming them.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: The horses no longer live in the stables, which instead is the place to go to buy gifts, books, our own jams and pickles.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: And clothes and blankets made of wool from our sheep.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: Outside the shed, which is the only brick building, you can climb into a horse-drawn carriage for a lovely relaxing tour of the park and farm.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: The carriages are well over 100 years old.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: And finally, the parkland, which was laid out in the 18th century, with a lake and trees that are now well established.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: You'll see types of cattle and sheep that are hardly ever found on farms these days.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: We're helping to preserve them to stop their numbers falling further.
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: OK, well, if you'd like to come with me, we will start...
utterances.speaker: NICK

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Many past owners made changes to
Sir Edward Downes built Oniton Hall because he wanted
options: a place to display his wealth.
a place for artists and writers.

items.title: Visitors can learn about the work of servants in the past from
What is new for children at Oniton Hall?

text: Which activity is offered at each of the following locations on the farm?
textReadable: Which activity is offered at each of the following locations on the farm?

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two Theater Studies students discussing stage and screen performances of Shakespeare's play Romeo and Juliet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 and 22.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 and 22.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Did you make notes while you were watching the performances of Romeo and Juliet,
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Gemma?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Yes, I did.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I found it quite hard though.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I kept getting too involved in the play.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Me too.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I ended up not taking notes.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I wrote down my impressions when I got home.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Do you mind if I check a few things with you, in case I've missed anything, and I've also got some questions about our assignment.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: No, it's good to talk things through.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I may have missed things too.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: OK, great.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: So first of all I'm not sure how much information we should include in our reviews.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Well, I don't think we need to describe what happens, especially as Romeo and Juliet is one of Shakespeare's most well-known plays.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Yeah, everyone knows the story.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: In an essay, we'd focus on the poetry and Shakespeare's use of imagery, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: But that isn't really relevant in a review.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: We're supposed to focus on how effective this particular production is.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Hmm, we should say what made it a success or a failure.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: And part of that means talking about the emotional impact the performance had on us.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I think that's important.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: And we should definitely mention how well the director handled important bits of the play, like when Romeo climbs onto Juliet's balcony.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: And the fight between Mercutio and Tybalt.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: It would also be interesting to mention the theater space, and how the director used it, but I don't think we'll have space in 800 words.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: No, OK.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: That all sounds quite straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 23 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 23 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, what about the Emporium Theater's production of the play?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I thought some things worked really well, but there were some problems too.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: What about the set for example?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I think it was visually really stunning.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I'd say that was probably the most memorable thing about this production.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: You're right.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: The set design was really amazing, but actually I have seen similar ideas used in other productions.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Hmm. What about the lighting?
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Some of the scenes were so dimly lit, it was quite hard to see.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I didn't dislike it.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: It helped to change the mood of the quieter scenes.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: That's a good point.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: What did you think of the costumes?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I was a bit surprised by the contemporary dress, I must say.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I think it worked well, but I had assumed it would be more conventional.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Me too.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I liked the music at the beginning, and I thought the musicians were brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: But I thought they were wasted, because the music didn't have much impact in Acts 2 and 3.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Yes, that was a shame.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: One problem with this production was that the actors didn't deliver the lines that well.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: They were speaking too fast.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: It was a problem I agree, but I thought it was because they weren't speaking loudly enough, especially at key points in the play.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I actually didn't have a problem with that.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: It's been an interesting experience watching different versions of Romeo and Juliet, hasn't it?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Definitely.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: It's made me realize how relevant the play still is.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: I mean a lot's changed since Shakespeare's time, but in many ways nothing's changed.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: There are always disagreements and tension between teenagers and their parents.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: That's something all young people can relate to, more than the violence and the extreme emotions in the play.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: How did you find watching it in translation?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I expected to find it more challenging, but I could follow the story pretty well.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I stopped worrying about not being able to understand all the words, and focused on the actors' expressions.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: The ending was pretty powerful.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Yes, that somehow intensified the emotion for me.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Did you know Shakespeare's been translated into more languages than any other writer?
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: What's the reason for his international appeal, do you think?
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: I was reading that it's because his plays are about basic themes that people everywhere are familiar with.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: And they can also be understood on different levels.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: The characters have such depth.
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: Which allows directors to experiment and find new angles.
audioscript.speaker: ED

audioscript.text: That's really important, because...
audioscript.speaker: GEMMA

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO things do the students agree they need to include in their reviews of Romeo and Juliet?

text: Which opinion do the speakers give about each of the following aspects of The Emporium's production of Romeo and Juliet?
textReadable: Which opinion do the speakers give about each of the following aspects of The Emporium's production of Romeo and Juliet?

list: They both expected this to be more traditional.
They both thought this was original.
They agree this created the right atmosphere.
They agree this was a major strength.
They were both disappointed by this.
They disagree about why this was an issue.
They disagree about how this could be improved.
body.title: Aspects of the production

items.title: The students think the story of Romeo and Juliet is still relevant for young people today because
options: it illustrates how easily conflict can start.
it deals with problems that families experience.

items.title: The students found watching Romeo and Juliet in another language
Why do the students think Shakespeare's plays have such international appeal?
options: They can be interpreted in many ways.

test_text.title: Impact of digital technology on Icelandic language

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a lecturer on a languages course talking about the impact of digital technology on Icelandic, the native language of Iceland.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's make a start.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Over the past few sessions, we've been considering the reasons why some world languages are in decline.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And today, I'm going to introduce another factor that affects languages, and the speakers of those languages, and that's technology, and in particular digital technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In order to illustrate its effect, I'm going to focus on the Icelandic language, which is spoken by around 321,000 people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Most of whom live in Iceland, an island in the North Atlantic Ocean.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The problem for this language is not the number of speakers, even though this number is small, nor is it about losing words to other languages such as English.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, the vocabulary of Icelandic is continually increasing, because when speakers need a new word for something, they tend to create one, rather than borrowing from another language.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All this makes Icelandic quite a special language.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's changed very little in the past millennium, yet it can handle 21st century concepts related to the use of computers and digital technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Take for example the word for web browser.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is vafri in Icelandic, which comes from the verb 'to wander'.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I can't think of a more appropriate term, because that's exactly what you do mentally when you browse the internet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then there's an Icelandic word for podcast, which is too hard to pronounce, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Icelandic then is alive and growing, but, and it's a big but, young Icelanders spend a great deal of time in the digital world, and this world is predominantly English.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Think about smartphones.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They didn't even exist until comparatively recently, but today young people use them all the time to read books, watch TV or films.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Play games, listen to music, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Obviously, this is a good thing in many respects, because it promotes their bilingual skills.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the extent of the influence of English in the virtual world is staggering.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it's all happening really fast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For their parents and grandparents, the change is less concerning, because they already have their native-speaker skills in Icelandic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But for young speakers, well, the outcome is a little troubling.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, teachers have found that playground conversations in Icelandic secondary schools can be conducted entirely in English, while teachers of much younger children have reported situations where their classes find it easier to say what is in a picture using English, rather than Icelandic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The very real and worrying consequence of all this, is that the young generation in Iceland is at risk of losing its mother tongue.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of course, this is happening to other European languages too, but while internet companies might be willing to offer say French options in their systems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's much harder for them to justify the expense of doing the same for a language that has a population the size of a French town, such as Nice.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The other drawback of Icelandic is the grammar, which is significantly more complex than in most languages.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the moment, the tech giants are simply not interested in tackling this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what is the Icelandic government doing about this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, large sums of money are being allocated to a language technology fund that it is hoped will lead to the development of Icelandic sourced apps.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And other social media and digital systems, but clearly this is going to be an uphill struggle.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On the positive side, they know that Icelandic is still the official language of education and government.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It has survived for well over 1,000 years, and the experts predict that its future in this nation state is sound, and will continue to be so.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, there's no doubt that it's becoming an inevitable second choice in young people's lives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This raises important questions when you consider how much of the past is tied up in a language.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Will young Icelanders lose their sense of their own identity?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another issue that concerns the government of Iceland is this. If children are learning two languages through different routes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Neither of which they are fully fluent in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Will they be able to express themselves properly?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear a lecturer on a languages course talking about the impact of digital technology on Icelandic, the native language of Iceland.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Right, everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's make a start.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Over the past few sessions, we've been considering the reasons why some world languages are in decline.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And today, I'm going to introduce another factor that affects languages, and the speakers of those languages, and that's technology, and in particular digital technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In order to illustrate its effect, I'm going to focus on the Icelandic language, which is spoken by around 321,000 people.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Most of whom live in Iceland, an island in the North Atlantic Ocean.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The problem for this language is not the number of speakers, even though this number is small, nor is it about losing words to other languages such as English.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, the vocabulary of Icelandic is continually increasing, because when speakers need a new word for something, they tend to create one, rather than borrowing from another language.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All this makes Icelandic quite a special language.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's changed very little in the past millennium, yet it can handle 21st century concepts related to the use of computers and digital technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Take for example the word for web browser.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is vafri in Icelandic, which comes from the verb 'to wander'.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I can't think of a more appropriate term, because that's exactly what you do mentally when you browse the internet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then there's an Icelandic word for podcast, which is too hard to pronounce, and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Icelandic then is alive and growing, but, and it's a big but, young Icelanders spend a great deal of time in the digital world, and this world is predominantly English.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Think about smartphones.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They didn't even exist until comparatively recently, but today young people use them all the time to read books, watch TV or films.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Play games, listen to music, and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Obviously, this is a good thing in many respects, because it promotes their bilingual skills.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the extent of the influence of English in the virtual world is staggering.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it's all happening really fast.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For their parents and grandparents, the change is less concerning, because they already have their native-speaker skills in Icelandic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But for young speakers, well, the outcome is a little troubling.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, teachers have found that playground conversations in Icelandic secondary schools can be conducted entirely in English, while teachers of much younger children have reported situations where their classes find it easier to say what is in a picture using English, rather than Icelandic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The very real and worrying consequence of all this, is that the young generation in Iceland is at risk of losing its mother tongue.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of course, this is happening to other European languages too, but while internet companies might be willing to offer say French options in their systems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's much harder for them to justify the expense of doing the same for a language that has a population the size of a French town, such as Nice.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The other drawback of Icelandic is the grammar, which is significantly more complex than in most languages.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At the moment, the tech giants are simply not interested in tackling this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what is the Icelandic government doing about this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, large sums of money are being allocated to a language technology fund that it is hoped will lead to the development of Icelandic sourced apps.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And other social media and digital systems, but clearly this is going to be an uphill struggle.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On the positive side, they know that Icelandic is still the official language of education and government.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It has survived for well over 1,000 years, and the experts predict that its future in this nation state is sound, and will continue to be so.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, there's no doubt that it's becoming an inevitable second choice in young people's lives.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This raises important questions when you consider how much of the past is tied up in a language.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Will young Icelanders lose their sense of their own identity?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another issue that concerns the government of Iceland is this. If children are learning two languages through different routes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Neither of which they are fully fluent in.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Will they be able to express themselves properly?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

has not changed a lot over the last thousand years
has its own words for computer-based concepts, such as web browser and <input>
items.title: The Icelandic language

are having discussions using only English while they are in the <input> at school
are better able to identify the content of a <input> in English than Icelandic
items.title: Young speakers

items: write very little in Icelandic because of the small number of speakers and because of how complicated its <input> is

items: has set up a fund to support the production of more digital content in the language
believes that Icelandic has a secure future
is worried that young Icelanders may lose their <input> as Icelanders
is worried about the consequences of children not being <input> in either Icelandic or English
items.title: The Icelandic government

body.title: The impact of digital technology on the Icelandic language
